card_title: Bank says no fee. You still paid $300.

videos.video_title: How banks charge you nothing and still keep $300

variants.vo: Pattern we see across SEA importers. Operator wires 10 grand to a Chinese supplier. The bank confirmation says zero fees. Zero. Then we look at the exchange rate they used. Banks have a 'real' rate they use with each other, and a rate they show you. The real one was 3 percent better. That's 300 dollars the bank quietly kept, hidden inside the rate. No fee on paper. 300 dollars off in the conversion. Same operator the next month, run through WorldFirst. The rate showed on screen before they confirmed. Almost the same as the real one. The 300 stayed in the order. Check out WorldFirst.

Quick maths. You wire 10K to your supplier. The bank says no fee. You believe them. Here's what they don't show you. Banks have a 'real' exchange rate they use among each other. They give you a slightly worse one and keep the difference. On this wire, the difference was 3 percent. 3 percent of 10 grand is 300 dollars. Gone. No line item. No notification. Run that across 12 wires a year. That's 3,600 dollars sitting in someone else's pocket. Search WorldFirst. We show the rate before you hit send.

Everyone tells you to find a bank with no wire fees. Truth is, the wire fee was never the cost. Banks charge you in two places. One you see, the 25 dollars on the confirmation. One you don't, baked into the exchange rate. They use a slightly worse rate than the real one and keep the gap. On 10 grand, that's around 300 dollars. The visible fee is 25. Which one are you actually negotiating? Look up WorldFirst. The math gets honest.

card_title: Three operators, same $50K wire, three different supplier-end amounts

videos.video_title: Three operators, same wire, three amounts

variants.vo: Three operators sent the same 50 grand wire to China last month. Three different amounts arrived. One supplier got 49,250. Another got 49,700. The third got 49,850. Same wire size. Same supplier. Same Tuesday. 600 dollars of spread, and all three confirmations said no fee. The difference was the exchange rate each provider used. Banks tell you you're getting a competitive rate, but the actual rate they use with each other is usually 1 to 2 percent better. WorldFirst is built closer to that real rate, so the conversion happens once at a price that's actually fair. Check out WorldFirst.

Three operators. 50,000 dollar wire. Same supplier. Same day. Three amounts arrived. Operator A's bank kept 1.5 percent in the rate, supplier got 49,250. Operator B's bank kept 0.6 percent, 49,700. Operator C used WorldFirst, kept 0.3 percent, 49,850. Same wire size, 600 dollars of difference. All three confirmations said no fee. The fee was inside the rate. Multiply 600 by 12 wires a year. That's 7,200 dollars that depends entirely on which provider you used. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone tells you the wire is the wire. Truth is, the wire is the rate. Three operators sent identical 50K wires to the same factory this week. Three different amounts arrived. The provider you picked decided how much of your money the supplier sees. Banks take a 1 to 1.5 percent cut on the exchange rate every time, that's 500 to 750 dollars off every 50K wire. WorldFirst takes about 0.3 percent. Same wire. Same supplier. The provider is the cost. Look up WorldFirst. We do this differently.

Around 2 percent. That's 12,000 dollars a year. Twelve grand that never shows up on a statement, never shows up on a confirmation, just sits inside the rate. The same operators on WorldFirst pay around 0.4 percent. On the same 600K, that's around 2,400. The 9,500 dollar gap goes back into the business. We also let you lock in tomorrow's rate today, so a wire 6 months out costs what you planned for. Check out WorldFirst.

card_title: Bank wire vs WorldFirst on the same $50K payment

videos.video_title: I sent $50K. My supplier got $48,500. Here is why.

variants.vo: Pattern we see every quarter. Operator wires 50 grand to their Chinese factory. WhatsApp pings the next day. Supplier says, we got 48,560. We pull the bank confirmation. The rate the bank used was 2.5 percent worse than the real rate banks use with each other. That's 1,250 dollars lost in the conversion. Then two banks in the middle of the wire each took 30 to 40 dollars to pass the money along. Supplier ends up 1,440 short. Operator wires again. New fee. New cut. Production delayed 4 days. Same operator next quarter, same 50K, through WorldFirst. Supplier gets 49,850. No banks in the middle, no second wire. Check out WorldFirst.

Quick maths. 50,000 dollar wire to your Chinese supplier. Bank route. Sending fee 50 dollars. The bank takes a 2 percent cut on the exchange rate, that's 1,000. Then two banks in the middle of the wire each deduct 30 to pass it along, 60 more. Supplier gets 48,890. Same 50K through WorldFirst. Direct route to China, no banks in the middle. Cut on the rate is 0.4 percent, 200 dollars. Flat transfer cost, nothing deducted along the way. Supplier gets 49,800. Difference, 910 dollars on a single wire. Same supplier. Same Tuesday. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone tells you a wire is a wire. Truth is, the provider decides everything. Bank wire on 50K to China. 2 percent cut on the exchange rate, 1,000 dollars. Operator spends three months negotiating the supplier down 2 percent on unit price. Feels like a win. Then we run the numbers on the wires. Every time the bank sent money abroad, they took a 4 percent cut on the exchange rate. On 500 grand of sourcing, that's 20,000 dollars a year. The 2 percent you negotiated off the supplier is half what the bank quietly took. WorldFirst takes around 0.4 percent. 18 grand stays in the business. The supplier negotiation wasn't the lever. The provider was. Check out WorldFirst.

card_title: How to read FX margin off your wire confirmation

videos.video_title: Find the hidden FX margin in 30 seconds

variants.vo: Pattern we see when operators check their own confirmations. Pull your last wire. Find the rate the bank used to convert your money, usually near the bottom. Say it's 7.05. Now Google the exchange rate for that day, that's the real one banks use with each other. Say it's 7.22. Run the math. 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times 100. That's 2.35 percent. On a 50K wire, that's 1,175 dollars the bank quietly took in the conversion. Didn't appear anywhere on the confirmation. Same operator runs the next wire through WorldFirst. The rate shows on screen before they confirm. No hunting. No Google. The cut is around 0.4 percent, in plain numbers. Check out WorldFirst.

Find your last wire confirmation. Locate the rate the bank used to convert your money, usually near the bottom. Google the exchange rate for that exact day, that's the real one banks use with each other. Now do the math. Real rate minus the rate they used, divided by the real rate, times 100. That's the bank's cut in percent. Times your wire amount equals what they took. Example. They used 7.05, the real rate was 7.22. Gap is 2.35 percent. On a 50K wire, that's 1,175 dollars. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you hit send, so there's no math to do later. Cut is around 0.4 percent. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone tells you to check the fee line on your wire confirmation. Truth is, the fee line is a decoy. The real cost is the exchange rate the bank used to convert your money. That's on the confirmation. The 'real' rate, the one banks use with each other, is free on Google. Subtract one from the other. That's what the bank kept. On most wires it's 2 to 3 percent. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars nobody listed. WorldFirst shows the rate before you confirm. The math is done in front of you. Look up WorldFirst. The math gets honest.

card_title: Good rate quoted vs real interbank rate

videos.video_title: What 'we'll give you a good rate' actually means

variants.vo: Pattern we see across operator accounts. Relationship manager at the bank calls. Says, we'll give you a good rate. Operator asks what good means. Answer is, better than retail. Operator asks the actual number when the wire goes out. Gets 7.05. We Google the real rate banks use with each other at that minute. It's 7.22. Gap is 2.4 percent. On a 100K wire, that's 2,400 dollars. The rate isn't bad. It just isn't what the relationship manager implied. Same operator moves wires to WorldFirst. The rate runs close to the real one, around 0.4 percent off. No relationship manager needed. The number speaks. Check out WorldFirst.

Quick maths. Your bank quotes you 7.05 on US dollars to Chinese yuan. Says it's a good rate. The real rate that banks use with each other, free on Google that same minute, is 7.22. Gap is 2.4 percent. On 10K, 240 dollars. On 100K, 2,400. Same percent, scales with wire size. WorldFirst runs around 0.4 percent off the real rate. On 100K, that's 400 dollars, not 2,400. Difference, 2,000 dollars per wire. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone tells you to ask your bank for a good rate. Truth is, when your bank says good rate, they mean good for them. The real exchange rate, the one banks use with each other, is free on Google. The 'good rate' your relationship manager quotes is usually 2 to 3 percent worse than that. On a 100K wire, that's 2,000 to 3,000 dollars they keep. Banks compete on the word 'good'. WorldFirst competes on the number. Around 0.4 percent off the real rate. Look up WorldFirst. We do this differently.
